data.name: Activities
data.name_path: Activities
data.description: Encompasses the actions, hobbies, pastimes, and events that characters perform or participate in throughout the story. This category covers a wide spectrum from everyday routines and domestic tasks to specialized skills, creative arts, sports, criminal acts, and leisure pursuits. It focuses on what characters do, rather than who they are or where they are.

data.description: The target demographic for which a work is primarily intended. data.name: Character Archetype
data.name_path: Character Archetype
data.description: Recurring personality patterns and recognizable role templates that define how a character behaves, thinks, or is perceived by the audience. These archetypes serve as cultural shorthand, drawing on established character models that shape expectations and narrative dynamics. They focus on the character’s core identity and typical behavior across different stories.

data.name: Antiguity

data.description:
**Era of Classical Empires (c. 3000 BCE - 500 CE):**
Spanning approximately 3500 years, this period saw Rome's Mediterranean empire and China's Han Dynasty unification. Japan's Yayoi period (300 BCE - 300 CE) developed rice cultivation and tribal states, while Korea's Three Kingdoms (57 BCE - 668 CE) competed for dominance. Characterized by foundational philosophies and early bureaucratic systems across civilizations.

data.name: 1st Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Antiguity > 1st Century
data.description:
## Years 1-100 CE
- **West:** Early Roman Empire (Pax Romana, Julio-Claudian dynasty)
- **Japan:** Yayoi Period (Tribal confederacies) Rice cultivation, bronze technology
- **China:** Late Han Dynasty (Eastern Han decline, silk road trade)
- **Korea:** Three Kingdoms Period (Goguryeo, Baekje, Silla competition)

data.name: 2nd Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Antiguity > 2nd Century
data.description:
## Years 101-200 CE
- **West:** Roman Empire (Antonine Golden Age, maximum expansion)
- **Japan:** Yayoi Period (Korean contact, iron tools introduction)
- **China:** Eastern Han Dynasty (Silk Road prosperity, paper invention)
- **Korea:** Three Kingdoms (Military conflicts, cultural development)

data.name: 3rd Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Antiguity > 3rd Century
data.description:
## Years 201-300 CE
- **West:** Crisis of the Third Century (Roman military anarchy)
- **Japan:** Kofun Period (Yamato state emergence, keyhole tombs)
- **China:** Three Kingdoms Period (Shu-Han falls, famous battles)
- **Korea:** Three Kingdoms (Goguryeo expansion, Buddhist influence)

data.name: 4th Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Antiguity > 4th Century
data.description:
## Years 301-400 CE
- **West:** Late Antiquity (Christianization, Constantinople founded)
- **Japan:** Kofun Period (Large tomb construction, state formation)
- **China:** Sixteen Kingdoms (Political fragmentation, northern invasions)
- **Korea:** Three Kingdoms (Buddhist adoption, cultural exchange)

data.name: 5th Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Antiguity > 5th Century
data.description:
## Years 401-500 CE
- **West:** Fall of Western Rome (Barbarian kingdoms emerge)
- **Japan:** Kofun Period (Imperial consolidation, Korean immigration)
- **China:** Northern and Southern Dynasties (Cultural division)
- **Korea:** Three Kingdoms Peak (Territorial expansion, diplomacy)

data.name: Early Modern Period
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Early Modern Period
data.description:
**Age of Discovery and Early Globalization (c. 1500 - 1800 CE):**
A 300-year transformative era where European colonial expansion coincided with China's Ming/Qing dynasties and Japan's Edo isolation (1603-1868). Korea's Joseon dynasty (1392-1897) maintained Confucian governance. This period established global trade networks and distinct national identities.

data.name: 16th Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Early Modern Period > 16th Century
data.description:
## Years 1501-1600 CE
- **West:** Age of Discovery (Columbian Exchange, Reformation)
- **Japan:** Sengoku Period (Civil war, Portuguese traders arrive)
- **China:** Late Ming (European contact, silver trade)
- **Korea:** Joseon Dynasty (Japanese invasions, turtle ships)

data.name: 17th Century
data.name_path: Themes > Time Period > Period Piece > Early Modern Period > 17th Century
data.description:
## Years 1601-1700 CE
- **West:** Scientific Revolution (Absolutism, colonial expansion)
- **Japan:** Edo Period (Tokugawa peace, isolation policy)
- **China:** Qing Establishment (Manchu rule, Ming loyalists)
- **Korea:** Joseon Dynasty (Manchu invasions, isolation)

data.name: 18th Century

data.description:

- **Japan:** Edo Period (Cultural development, merchant class rise)
- **China:** High Qing (Prosperity, territorial expansion)
- **Korea:** Joseon Dynasty (Silhak movement, economic reforms)

data.name: Future

data.description:
**Horizon of Technological Transformation (22nd Century onward):**
The coming centuries project AI integration and space exploration involving all regions. This forward-looking era represents humanity's collective advancement toward new frontiers of existence, with potential rebalancing of global influence between established and emerging technological centers.

data.description:
**Age of Feudalism and Courtly Civilizations (c. 500 - 1500 CE):**
Covering a millennium, Europe developed feudal systems while China experienced Tang/Song golden ages. Japan's Heian period (794-1185) refined courtly culture as Korea's Goryeo dynasty (918-1392) advanced Buddhist scholarship. This thousand-year era featured religious expansion and sophisticated aristocratic traditions.

data.name: Publication Medium
data.name_path: Work Info > Publication Medium
data.description:
**Publication Medium** refers to the channel or context through which a work is released and made available to readers.  
It identifies *how* and *where* the content is published, rather than its origin (cultural source) or format (structural style).  
This category captures whether a work is distributed professionally, independently, physically, or digitally.

### Subcategories
- **Magazine**  
  - Serialized chapters published periodically in print or digital magazines. Often includes multiple works in one issue.  

- **Editorial**  
  - Formal collected volumes (tankōbon, graphic novels, e‑books). Released by professional publishers, usually after serialization.  The distinction is primarily for works that were released directly without prior serialization.

- **Web**  
  - Official/professional digital platforms (e.g., Webtoon, MangaPlus, KakaoPage). Designed for mass distribution and often serialized.  

- **Doujinshi**  
  - Self‑published or independent works.  Printed and sold at conventions or specialty shops or distributed via platforms like Pixiv, Booth, DLsite, FANBOX.
